verses.text: That night the king cannot sleep, has the chronicles read, and learns that Mordecai's life-saving loyalty was never rewarded. A sleepless night is no accident. God who governs even a king's restlessness brings the forgotten deed to light at the perfect hour.

That night the king cannot sleep, has the chronicles read, and learns that Mordecai's life-saving loyalty was never rewarded. A sleepless night is no accident. God who governs even a king's restlessness brings the forgotten deed to light at the perfect hour.

That night the king cannot sleep, has the chronicles read, and learns that Mordecai's life-saving loyalty was never rewarded. A sleepless night is no accident. God who governs even a king's restlessness brings the forgotten deed to light at the perfect hour.

Just then Haman arrives, intending to ask for Mordecai's hanging, and the king asks who is in the court. Their paths cross by no chance. Providence so times events that the schemer walks straight into the unravelling of his own plot.

Just then Haman arrives, intending to ask for Mordecai's hanging, and the king asks who is in the court. Their paths cross by no chance. Providence so times events that the schemer walks straight into the unravelling of his own plot.

Asked how to honour the man the king delights in, Haman, sure it must be himself, proposes royal robes, the king's horse, and a noble herald. Vanity overreaches itself. Pride, asked to dream, only exposes how high it has set its own heart.

Asked how to honour the man the king delights in, Haman, sure it must be himself, proposes royal robes, the king's horse, and a noble herald. Vanity overreaches itself. Pride, asked to dream, only exposes how high it has set its own heart.

'Make haste,' says the king, 'and do so to Mordecai the Jew' — and Haman must lead his enemy through the city, proclaiming his honour. The trap snaps shut on its maker. God can make the proud the very servant of the man he hoped to destroy.

'Make haste,' says the king, 'and do so to Mordecai the Jew' — and Haman must lead his enemy through the city, proclaiming his honour. The trap snaps shut on its maker. God can make the proud the very servant of the man he hoped to destroy.

Mordecai returns to the gate while Haman hurries home mourning; his wife and counsellors warn he will surely fall before this Jew, even as the king's eunuchs hasten him to the banquet. His ruin is already foreseen. When God begins to bring a man down, all his comforts turn to omens of his end.
